retrieved_docs.title: Glanzmann Thrombasthenia -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Describe the pathophysiology of Glanzmann thrombasthenia. Descibe the diagnostic options for evaluation of suspected Glanzmann thrombasthenia. Explain the treatment options for Glanzmann thrombasthenia. Identify the importance of an interprofessional team to optimize outcomes for patients with Glanzmann thrombasthenia. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Glanzmann Thrombasthenia -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Glanzmann thrombasthenia is a congenital bleeding disorder caused by a deficiency of the platelet integrin alpha IIb beta3. This integrin is the platelet fibrinogen receptor and is thus essential to platelet aggregation and hemostasis. Patients with Glanzmann thrombasthenia have lifelong bleeding episodes that often involve the mucocutaneous membranes. This activity reviews the clinical presentation, evaluation, and treatment options for Glanzmann thrombasthenia and highlights the role of the interprofessional team in caring for patients with this condition.

retrieved_docs.title: Glanzmann Thrombasthenia -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with GT should be educated on how to recognize abnormal bleeding. Additionally, they should be instructed to avoid unnecessary trauma and not to take medications that interfere with platelet function. The importance of dental hygiene should be stressed as well. Some patient subgroups require special attention, i.e., young female patients may need instructions for heavy menstrual bleeding. Family planning discussions, including the risk of childbirth and affected children, are important for some patients as well. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Glanzmann Thrombasthenia -- Review Questions
retrieved_docs.content: Access free multiple choice questions on this topic. Click here for a simplified version. Comment on this article.

retrieved_docs.title: Glanzmann Thrombasthenia -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: GT is classified into three types based on the function and expression (compared to non-GT platelets) of the alpha IIb beta 3 integrin. Less than 5% expression of the integrin corresponds to Type I GT, 5% to 20% expression corresponds to type II GT, and greater than 20% expression with dysfunction corresponds to variant-type GT. Variant-type GT is rare. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Glanzmann Thrombasthenia -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: GT is a platelet disorder, and platelets are identified on blood smears. In general, the platelets of a patient with GT appear normal, but rare mutations can result in platelets that are of unequal size. [4] [5] Isolated families with GT have been identified as having large and relatively few platelets, a condition known as macrothrombocytopenia. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Glanzmann Thrombasthenia -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Glanzmann thrombasthenia (GT), first described in 1918, is a congenital bleeding disorder caused by a defect and/or deficiency of a platelet integrin, alpha IIb beta3. [1] [2] The integrin is the platelet fibrinogen receptor and essential to platelet aggregation and hemostasis. [3] Patients with this disorder have lifelong bleeding episodes that often involve the mucocutaneous membranes [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Glanzmann Thrombasthenia -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Secondary tests may include clot retraction, which is impaired in GT. Molecular genetic tests are included in the ISTH guidance for cases that remain undiagnosed after other laboratory studies. Gene mutations in ITGA2B or ITGB3 are identified in GT. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Glanzmann Thrombasthenia -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: GT is an autosomal recessive disorder with mutations involving chromosome 17q21, specifically the ITGA2B or ITGB3 genes. [2] [4] [5] Mutations of either gene can cause GT, and hundreds of mutations have been reported. GT results when a patient is homozygous for the same mutation or is a compound heterozygote for different mutations. [3] The various genetic alterations can lead to different levels of function and expression of the alpha IIb beta3 integrin. [2] The Manouche mutation has no alpha IIb beta3 integrin expression. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Glanzmann Thrombasthenia -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: A voluntary Glanzmann thrombasthenia registry enrolled patients with GT, and their data indicates that the first symptoms occurred at a median age of 1one year and a mean of 5.6 years. Of the enrollees, 85% were diagnosed with GT by 14 years of age. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Glanzmann Thrombasthenia -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Appropriate athletic activities, vocational training, and other psychosocial issues can also be addressed. [6] Interprofessional discussions are essential to operative planning, as well. Surgeons, anesthesiologists, and transfusion medicine physicians can help optimize perioperative management. [10] Only through such a team approach can the morbidity of GT be reduced.

retrieved_docs.title: Glanzmann Thrombasthenia -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: The prevalence of GT is estimated to be about 1:1,000,000 in the general population. [2] In certain areas, such as those with high consanguinity, the prevalence is roughly 1:200,000 or higher. [3] [6] Pakistan and the Canadian provinces Newfoundland and Labrador are some of the areas with a high prevalence. [9] [6] Some patients may have mild symptoms and are never identified as having GT, so the actual prevalence may be higher than reported. [6] Studies show that females are slightly more frequently affected than males. GT is commonly encountered in children and young adults but can affect any age. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Glanzmann Thrombasthenia -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: GT is a severe bleeding disorder, and life-threatening or fatal bleeding can occur spontaneously or with invasive procedures, severe trauma, or as a consequence of childbirth. [3] [6] The broad mutational causes of GT yield many clinical presentations that range in severity. Patients can do well with healthcare team preparation and coordination, such as the 52-year-old man with GT who had a successful open aortic valve replacement. [10] In general, GT bleeding episodes are not as life-threatening as those associated with inherited coagulation disorders. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Glanzmann Thrombasthenia -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: The alpha IIb beta3 integrin, formerly known as GPIIb-IIIa, is the platelet’s fibrinogen receptor. When platelets are activated, the alpha IIb beta3 integrin shifts into its active configuration to allow fibrinogen binding. As platelets bind the fibrinogen, they aggregate and provide primary hemostasis. Without functioning fibrinogen receptors, or without enough of them, bleeding can be spontaneous or occur with an injury. [2] [7] Additionally, platelets in GT are not as efficient at generating thrombin, an essential part of converting fibrinogen to fibrin. [3] Secondary hemostasis occurs when cross-linked fibrin stabilizes the platelet plug, and is thus also affected by GT. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Glanzmann Thrombasthenia -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: An interprofessional approach involving specialized nursing, social workers, and physical therapy can promote recognition of abnormal bleeding and teach techniques for self-management. Nurses looking after patients post-surgery should monitor for bleeding and ensure that rFVIIa is available in the blood bank

retrieved_docs.title: Glanzmann Thrombasthenia -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with GT and chronic mild bleeding may develop iron deficiency anemia, but the majority of GT complications are related to its treatment rather than the disease itself. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1751
retrieved_docs.content: Factor VIII may be used to treat coagulation and hemorrhagic disorders, as it helps correct deficiencies in the clotting cascade that underlie these bleeding conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 605
retrieved_docs.content: Direct Factor Xa Inhibitors are classified as antithrombotic agents and function through the inhibition of coagulation factor Xa, a key step in the clotting cascade. This class of drugs exerts its therapeutic effect by directly targeting factor Xa activity, thereby reducing thrombus formation. Rivaroxaban is a specific medication that belongs to the Direct Factor Xa Inhibitors class, exemplifying the use of this mechanism in clinical practice for the prevention and treatment of thromboembolic disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 2058
retrieved_docs.content: Human factor VIII is used as a therapeutic agent to treat coagulation and hemorrhagic disorders, helping to correct deficient clotting activity and control bleeding. By supplementing the missing or insufficient factor VIII, it restores normal hemostasis in conditions such as hemophilia A and other bleeding disorders, thereby reducing the risk of severe hemorrhage and improving patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2732
retrieved_docs.content: Kyselina tranexamicová, an antifibrinolytic medication, is contraindicated in patients with abnormalities of the faktory krevní koagulace (blood coagulation factors). Because tranexamic acid can promote clot stability, its use should be avoided in individuals who have deficiencies or disorders of these coagulation factors, as it may increase the risk of thrombotic complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2324
retrieved_docs.content: Argatroban belongs to the class of direct thrombin inhibitors, a group of anticoagulant agents that exert their therapeutic effect by directly inhibiting the enzyme thrombin, thereby preventing the conversion of fibrinogen to fibrin and reducing clot formation. This classification underscores argatroban’s mechanism of action and its role in managing conditions that require precise anticoagulation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 1 -- Community 225
retrieved_docs.content: and reagents, are essential for visualizing vascular structures in these procedures and in related imaging studies such as contrast‑enhanced computed tomography of the thorax. Together, heparin‑based anticoagulants prevent new clot formation, antithrombin III amplifies their effect, fibrinolytic enzymes actively dissolve existing thrombi, and regulatory proteins fine‑tune the coagulation cascade, while contrast‑enhanced imaging provides precise localization and monitoring, forming an integrated diagnostic‑therapeutic framework that optimizes patient care in thrombotic disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 4 -- Community 2694
retrieved_docs.content: Dextran 70, a high‑molecular‑weight plasma volume expander, is contraindicated in patients with الاعتلال الخثري (thrombotic disease). Because Dextran 70 can increase blood viscosity and promote coagulation, its use in individuals who already have a propensity for clot formation may exacerbate the underlying thrombotic condition and increase the risk of serious vascular complications. Clinicians should therefore avoid administering Dextran 70 to patients diagnosed with الاعتلال الخثري and consider alternative volume‑expanding therapies that do not carry this pro‑thrombotic risk.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2539
retrieved_docs.content: Argatroban and dabigatran etexilát are both members of the pharmacologic class known as direct thrombin inhibitors, agents that achieve anticoagulation by binding directly to thrombin and preventing the conversion of fibrinogen to fibrin. This classification highlights their shared mechanism of action and therapeutic role in conditions requiring precise inhibition of thrombin activity.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2737
retrieved_docs.content: Dextran 75, a high‑molecular‑weight plasma volume expander, is contraindicated in patients with الاعتلال الخثري (thrombotic disease); using dextran 75 in this condition may promote clot formation and worsen clinical outcomes, so clinicians should avoid prescribing it to individuals who have thrombotic disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 2 -- Community 620
retrieved_docs.content: Direct Factor Xa Inhibitors and Direct Thrombin Inhibitors are both subclasses of antithrombotic agents. Direct Factor Xa Inhibitors exert their therapeutic effect by inhibiting the activity of factor Xa, a key enzyme in the coagulation cascade, and this class includes the drug rivaroxaban. Direct Thrombin Inhibitors prevent clot formation by directly blocking thrombin, and argatroban is a representative member of this class. By targeting different points in the coagulation pathway, these two groups of antithrombotic agents provide complementary strategies for preventing and treating thrombotic disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 4 -- Community 2852
retrieved_docs.content: Dextran 40, a low‑molecular‑weight plasma volume expander, is contraindicated in the presence of الاعتلال الخثري (thrombotic disease). Because Dextran 40 can increase blood viscosity and promote clot formation, its use in patients with thrombotic disorders may exacerbate the underlying condition and raise the risk of further vascular occlusion. Consequently, clinicians should avoid administering Dextran 40 to individuals diagnosed with الاعتلال الخثري and consider alternative volume‑expanding therapies that do not carry this risk.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 1339
retrieved_docs.content: BLOOD AND BLOOD FORMING ORGAN DRUGS constitute a broad therapeutic class that includes antithrombotic agents; antithrombotic agents are a specific subclass of these drugs and are used to inhibit platelet aggregation and coagulation, thereby preventing the formation of pathological blood clots in various clinical settings.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1882
retrieved_docs.content: Human factor VIII, also referred to as faktor VIII, is used as a therapeutic agent for coagulation and hemorrhagic disorders. By supplying the deficient clotting protein, factor VIII, human helps restore normal hemostasis and control bleeding in patients affected by these disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 2 -- Community 1195
retrieved_docs.content: Direct Factor Xa Inhibitors and Direct Thrombin Inhibitors are both classes of antithrombotic agents. Direct Factor Xa Inhibitors exert their therapeutic effect through inhibition of factor Xa, a key enzyme in the coagulation cascade. Rivaroxaban is a specific drug that belongs to the Direct Factor Xa Inhibitor class. Direct Thrombin Inhibitors act by directly blocking thrombin activity, thereby preventing fibrin formation. Argatroban and dabigatran etexilate are individual agents that are members of the Direct Thrombin Inhibitor class. Together, these drug families provide targeted anticoagulation strategies for the prevention and treatment of thrombotic disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 2 -- Community 316
retrieved_docs.content: Direct thrombin inhibitors and Heparin group antithrombotic drugs are both classified as antithrombotic agents, which are used to prevent pathological blood clot formation. Within the class of direct thrombin inhibitors, argatroban is a specific member that exerts its antithrombotic effect by directly inhibiting thrombin activity. The Heparin group antithrombotic drugs comprise several distinct members, including antithrombin III, dalteparin, nadroparin, and tinzaparin, each of which enhances the activity of antithrombin III to inhibit clotting factors. Together, these drug classes and their individual agents represent key therapeutic options for managing and preventing thrombotic disorders in clinical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 1 -- Community 24
retrieved_docs.content: class. Collectively, these classifications illustrate the mechanistic relationships among hereditary‑angioedema therapies, heme‑related products, heparin derivatives, protein antithrombins, low‑molecular‑weight heparins, enzyme‑based fibrinolytics, other antithrombotic agents, and hemostatics, as well as their shared adverse‑effect considerations and relevance for patient management.
